nodes.label: Geometric Explanation of the Likelihood OOD Detection Paradox
nodes.summary: Kamkari et al. (ICML 2024) explain why likelihood-based deep generative models (normalizing flows, diffusion) assign higher likelihoods to OOD samples from simpler datasets yet never generate them. Core claim: high-density regions can contain minimal probability mass when data concentrates on lower-dimensional manifolds. The paradox is resolvable by pairing model likelihood with local intrinsic dimension (LID) estimated from the pre-trained DGM; the resulting detector matches/surpasses SOTA using the same DGM backbone.
nodes.source_file: raw/A Geometric Explanation of the Likelihood OOD Detection Paradox.pdf

nodes.label: Manifold hypothesis and the density-vs-mass distinction
nodes.summary: Natural data lies on low-dimensional submanifolds of R^d. A density p_theta may be highly peaked around an OOD manifold of lower dimension than the training manifold, yielding high likelihood yet near-zero probability mass because the manifold has negligible d-dimensional volume. Example: FMNIST-trained DGM peaked around a 1D MNIST manifold in 2D shows high density but vanishing mass. This separates 'where the model puts density' from 'what the model actually samples', reconciling the two-pronged paradox.
nodes.source_file: raw/A Geometric Explanation of the Likelihood OOD Detection Paradox.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section 1 + Fig. 1 + Section 2 Local Intrinsic Dimension

nodes.label: LID-via-score-rank estimator (Jacobian/score quantity)
nodes.summary: The geometric quantity that separates ID from OOD is the local intrinsic dimension LID_theta(x) of the model-implied manifold M_theta at x. Following Stanczuk et al., for a variance-exploding score-based DM one runs k independent noised trajectories from x up to small t_0 and evaluates the score s_theta(x_i, t_0); these lie in the normal space to M_theta at x. The matrix S(x)=[s_theta(x_1,t_0)|...|s_theta(x_k,t_0)] in R^{d x k} has rank equal to the normal-space dimension, and LID_theta(x) is approx d - rank S(x). For normalizing flows, the log-det Jacobian of f_theta plays the analogous geometric role.
nodes.source_file: raw/A Geometric Explanation of the Likelihood OOD Detection Paradox.pdf

nodes.label: Dual-criterion OOD detector: high likelihood AND high LID
nodes.summary: A datum is classified in-distribution only if it (i) has high model likelihood p_theta(x) and (ii) lies in a region with non-negligible probability mass, operationalized as large LID_theta(x) estimated from a single pre-trained DGM. Applied to normalizing flows and variance-exploding DMs, it matches or beats SOTA OOD-detection benchmarks (FMNIST vs MNIST/Omniglot, CIFAR10/CelebA vs SVHN) while using the same DGM backbone as baselines.
nodes.source_file: raw/A Geometric Explanation of the Likelihood OOD Detection Paradox.pdf
